volumeTitle: Antigone. The Women of Trachis. Philoctetes. Oedipus at Colonus
label: Philoctetes (Section 1.135 - Section 317)

sections.label: Section 1.135
sections.original: τί χρὴ τί χρή με , δέσποτʼ , ἐν ξένᾳ ξένον
sections.translation: I am a stranger in a strange land , Master . What must I hide , what must I reveal to a man who will be swift to suspicion ? Show me the way ! His skill excels all other skill , his wisdom has no equal , whose hands govern the godlike

sections.label: Section 1.140
sections.original: Διὸς σκῆπτρον ἀνάσσεται .
sections.translation: scepter given by Zeus . To you , my son , that sovereign power has descended from the dawn of time . Therefore tell me how I must serve you .

sections.label: Section 144
sections.original: νῦν μέν , ἴσως γὰρ τόπον ἐσχατιαῖς
sections.translation: For the present — since perhaps you wish to see the place

sections.label: Section 145
sections.original: προσιδεῖν ἐθέλεις ὅντινα κεῖται ,
sections.translation: on the island ’ s edge where he resides — survey it without fear . But when the dread traveler , who has left this dwelling , returns , step forward at my signal from time to time , and try to help as the moment may require .

sections.label: Section 1.150
sections.original: μέλον πάλαι μέλημά μοι λέγεις , ἄναξ ,
sections.translation: Long have I been careful of this care , my prince : that my eyes should be watchful for your good above all else . And now tell me , in what manner of shelter does he keep his dwelling ? In what region is he now ? It is not inopportune for

sections.label: Section 1.155
sections.original: μαθεῖν οὐκ ἀποκαίριον ,
sections.translation: me to learn so that he may not come upon me unawares from somewhere . In what place does he wander , or rest ? Does he plant his steps within his shelter , or abroad ?

sections.label: Section 159
sections.original: οἶκον μὲν ὁρᾷς τόνδʼ ἀμφίθυρον
sections.translation: Well , here you see his home with its two portals ,

sections.label: Section 160
sections.original: πετρίνης κοίτης .
sections.translation: his rocky cell .

sections.label: Section 161
sections.original: ποῦ γὰρ ὁ τλήμων αὐτὸς ἄπεστιν ;
sections.translation: And its wretched occupant , where is he gone to ?

sections.label: Section 162
sections.original: δῆλον ἔμοιγʼ ὡς φορβῆς χρείᾳ
sections.translation: It seems clear to me , anyway , that he is plowing his way along somewhere near here in search of food . For I know of a report that

sections.label: Section 165
sections.original: λόγος ἐστὶ φύσιν , θηροβολοῦντα
sections.translation: this is his means of sustenance — a wretch wretchedly shooting prey with his feathered shafts — and that no one comes near to him to heal his misery .

sections.label: Section 2.169
sections.original: οἰκτίρω νιν ἔγωγʼ , ὅπως ,
sections.translation: For my part , I pity him when I think how ,

sections.label: Section 2.170
sections.original: μή του κηδομένου βροτῶν
sections.translation: with no one to care for him , and seeing no companion ’ s face , but suffering eternally alone , he is plagued by fierce disease and bewildered by each need as it arises .

sections.label: Section 2.175
sections.original: χρείας ἱσταμένῳ . πῶς ποτε πῶς δύσμορος ἀντέχει ;
sections.translation: How , how does he endure his bitter fate ? Ah , contrivances of the gods ! Ah , unhappy tribes of mortals , whose life-portion exceeds due measure !

sections.label: Section 2.180
sections.original: οὗτος πρωτογόνων ἴσως
sections.translation: That man — inferior in no way , probably , to any man belonging to the oldest families — lies alone without companions and stripped of all life ’ s gifts

sections.label: Section 2.185
sections.original: θηρῶν , ἔν τʼ ὀδύναις ὁμοῦ
sections.translation: among the dappled or shaggy beasts . He is a man to be pitied for his torments and his hunger alike , enduring anguish that has no cure . But to his bitter cries the mountain nymph , babbling Echo , coming from afar ,

sections.label: Section 2.190
sections.original: οἰμωγαῖς ὑπακούει .
sections.translation: gives answer .

sections.label: Section 191
sections.original: οὐδὲν τούτων θαυμαστὸν ἐμοί ·
sections.translation: No part of this is a marvel to me . God-sent — if a man such as I may judge — are both those sufferings which attacked him from savage Chryse ,

sections.label: Section 195
sections.original: καὶ νῦν ἃ πονεῖ δίχα κηδεμόνων ,
sections.translation: and those with which he now toils untended . Surely he toils by the plan of some god so that he may not bend against Troy the invincible arrows divine , until the time be fulfilled at which , men say ,

sections.label: Section 200
sections.original: χρῆναί σφʼ ὑπὸ τῶνδε δαμῆναι .
sections.translation: by those arrows Troy is fated to fall .

sections.label: Section 3.201
sections.original: εὔστομʼ ἔχε , παῖ .
sections.translation: Hush , boy !

sections.label: Section 3.201a
sections.original: τί τόδε ;
sections.translation: What is it ?

sections.label: Section 3.201b
sections.original: προυφάνη κτύπος ,
sections.translation: I heard a sudden thud , one that might naturally come from a man worn by pain . From there it came , I think — or there .

sections.label: Section 3.205
sections.original: βάλλει βάλλει μʼ ἐτύμα
sections.translation: It strikes , strikes hard on my ear , the sure sound of someone creeping along his way as if tortured . I cannot miss that grievous cry of a man hard-pressed , even from afar — its tone is too clear .

sections.label: Section 3.210
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἔχε , τέκνον ,
sections.translation: But listen , son —

sections.label: Section 3.211
sections.original: λέγʼ ὅ τι .
sections.translation: To what ? Tell me .

sections.label: Section 3.212
sections.original: φροντίδας νέας .
sections.translation: To my latest thoughts . For he is not far from his home , but nearby . And not with music of the flute , like a shepherd pasturing his flocks , does he come ,—

sections.label: Section 3.215
sections.original: ὡς ποιμὴν ἀγροβότας , ἀλλʼ ἤ που πταίων ὑπʼ ἀνάγκας
sections.translation: no — but crying out a far-sounding howl as he stumbles , perhaps , from tortuous pain , or as he scans the haven unvisited by any ship . His cries are loud , and terrible .

sections.label: Section 219
sections.original: ἰὼ ξένοι ,
sections.translation: O strangers !

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: τίνες ποτʼ ἐς γῆν τήνδε κἀκ ποίας πάτρας
sections.translation: Who can you be , and from what country , that you have put into this harborless and desolate land ? What would I rightly say is your city or your ancestry ? The fashion of your equipment is Greek , and most welcome to my

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: φωνῆς δʼ ἀκοῦσαι βούλομαι · καὶ μή μʼ ὄκνῳ
sections.translation: sight ; but it would please me to hear your voices . And do not shrink from me in fear , or be frightened by my savage looks . No , pity one so wretched and so lonely , a castaway , so friendless and so miserable . Speak to me , if indeed you have come as friends .

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἀνταμείψασθʼ · οὐ γὰρ εἰκὸς οὔτʼ ἐμὲ
sections.translation: Oh , answer ! It is not right that I be disappointed by you in this request , at least , nor you by me .

sections.label: Section 232
sections.original: ἀλλʼ , ὦ ξένʼ , ἴσθι τοῦτο πρῶτον , οὕνεκα
sections.translation: Well , know first , stranger , that we are Greeks , since you desire to learn this .

sections.label: Section 234
sections.original: ὦ φίλτατον φώνημα · φεῦ τὸ καὶ λαβεῖν
sections.translation: O cherished sound ! Ah , that I should truly be

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: πρόσφθεγμα τοιοῦδʼ ἀνδρὸς ἐν χρόνῳ μακρῷ .
sections.translation: greeted by such a man , after so long a time ! What need , young man , has made you land here and brought you to this spot ? What business ? What wind so kind ? Speak , tell me all , so that I may know who you are .

sections.label: Section 239
sections.original: ἐγὼ γένος μέν εἰμι τῆς περιρρύτου
sections.translation: My birthplace is the island Scyros , and I am sailing

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: Σκύρου · πλέω δʼ ἐς οἶκον · αὐδῶμαι δὲ παῖς
sections.translation: homeward . I am the son of Achilles , by name Neoptolemus . Now you know everything .

sections.label: Section 242
sections.original: ὦ φιλτάτου παῖ πατρός , ὦ φίλης χθονός ,
sections.translation: O son of a father I loved , and of soil I cherished ! Ward of aged Lycomedes , on what mission have you touched this shore ? From where are you sailing ?

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: ἐξ Ἰλίου τοι δὴ τανῦν γε ναυστολῶ .
sections.translation: Well , since you ask , it is from Ilium that I am now guiding my ship .

sections.label: Section 246
sections.original: πῶς εἶπας ; οὐ γὰρ δὴ σύ γʼ ἦσθα ναυβάτης
sections.translation: What ? You were certainly not our shipmate at the beginning of the expedition there .

sections.label: Section 248
sections.original: ἦ γὰρ μετέσχες καὶ σὺ τοῦδε τοῦ πόνου ;
sections.translation: And did you have a part in that toil ?

sections.label: Section 249
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον , οὐ γὰρ οἶσθά μʼ ὅντινʼ εἰσορᾷς ;
sections.translation: Then you do not know who I am ?

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: πῶς γὰρ κάτοιδʼ ὅν γʼ εἶδον οὐδεπώποτε ;
sections.translation: How should I know one whom I have never seen before ?

sections.label: Section 251
sections.original: οὐδʼ ὄνομʼ ἄρʼ οὐδὲ τῶν ἐμῶν κακῶν κλέος
sections.translation: Then you have not even heard my name , or any rumor of those sufferings under which I have been perishing ?

sections.label: Section 253
sections.original: ὡς μηδὲν εἰδότʼ ἴσθι μʼ ὧν ἀνιστορεῖς .
sections.translation: Be sure that I know nothing of what you ask .

sections.label: Section 254
sections.original: ὦ πόλλʼ ἐγὼ μοχθηρός , ὦ πικρὸς θεοῖς ,
sections.translation: How cursed I must be , how abhorred by the gods ,

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: οὗ μηδὲ κληδὼν ὧδʼ ἔχοντος οἴκαδε
sections.translation: if not a word of my miserable condition has reached my homeland , or any Greek land at all ! Instead those men , who against the gods ’ laws cast me away , keep their secret and laugh , while my plague has ever flourished and grows worse !

sections.label: Section 260
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον , ὦ παῖ πατρὸς ἐξ Ἀχιλλέως ,
sections.translation: O my son , boy whose father was Achilles , here I am before you , the man of whom you have perhaps heard as lord of the bow of Heracles , Philoctetes the son of Poeas . I am he whom the two marshalls and the Cephallenian king

sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: ἔρριψαν αἰσχρῶς ὧδʼ ἔρημον , ἀγρίᾳ
sections.translation: shamelessly hurled to this solitude which you see , when I was wasting with a fierce disease , stricken by the savage bite of the murderous serpent . With that plague for my sole companion , boy , those men put me out

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: Χρύσης κατέσχον δεῦρο ναυβάτῃ στόλῳ .
sections.translation: here alone and left , after they landed here with their fleet from sea-washed Chryse . Delighted they were then , when they saw me asleep after much tossing on the waves , in the shelter of a cave upon the shore , and they abandoned me , first setting out a few rags , as though for an unfortunate beggar , and a bit of food , too —

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: ἐπωφέλημα σμικρόν , οἷʼ αὐτοῖς τύχοι .
sections.translation: a small work of charity . But may they get what they gave me ! Can you imagine , boy , what kind of awakening I had when they had gone , and I rose from sleep that day ?— what stinging tears I wept , and what miseries I bewailed when I saw that the ships with which I had sailed

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: πάσας βεβώσας , ἄνδρα δʼ οὐδένʼ ἔντοπον ,
sections.translation: were all gone , and that there was no man in the place , not one to help me , not one to ease the sickness that afflicted me , when looking all around me , I could find nothing at hand , save agony — but of that a ready store ?

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: ὁ μὲν χρόνος δὴ διὰ χρόνου προύβαινέ μοι ,
sections.translation: So time passed for me , season by season , and alone in this narrow house , I had to attend to all my wants by my own resources . For my stomach ’ s needs this bow provided , bringing down doves on the wing . And whatever my string-sped arrow might strike ,

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: νευροσπαδὴς ἄτρακτος , αὐτὸς ἂν τάλας
sections.translation: in pain I would crawl to it myself , dragging my wretched foot behind me . Or , again , if water had to be fetched , or , if when the frost had spread , as often happens in winter , a bit of firewood had to be broken , I would creep out in pain and

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: ἐμηχανώμην · εἶτα πῦρ ἂν οὐ παρῆν ,
sections.translation: manage it . Then fire would be lacking ; but by rubbing stone hard on stone I would at last reveal the hidden spark which preserves me from day to day . Indeed , a roof over my head and a fire inside provides all that I want — except release from my disease .

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: φέρʼ , ὦ τέκνον , νῦν καὶ τὸ τῆς νήσου μάθῃς .
sections.translation: Come now , son , you must understand what sort of island this is . No mariner approaches it by choice , since there is no anchorage or port where he can find a gainful market or a kindly host . This is not a place to which prudent men voyage . But suppose that some one has put in against his will , for such things may often

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: τάχʼ οὖν τις ἄκων ἔσχε · πολλὰ γὰρ τάδε
sections.translation: happen in the long course of a man ’ s life . These visitors , then , when they come , son , have compassionate words for me , and , perhaps moved by pity , they give me a little food or some clothing .

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: ἐκεῖνο δʼ οὐδείς , ἡνίκʼ ἂν μνησθῶ , θέλει ,
sections.translation: But there is one thing that no one will do , whenever I mention it : take me home in safety . No , this is already the tenth year that I am wasted by misery from hunger and suffering , by feeding this gluttonous plague . This is what the Atreids and the forceful Odysseus have done to me , boy .

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: ὦ παῖ , δεδράκασʼ , οἷʼ Ὀλύμπιοι θεοὶ
sections.translation: May the gods on Olympus someday give them agonies as strong in requital for mine !

sections.label: Section 317
sections.original: ἔοικα κἀγὼ τοῖς ἀφιγμένοις ἴσα
sections.translation: I believe that I , too , pity you , son of Poeas , as much as your former visitors .
